root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

risks and losses

Theoretical risk minimization

We assume that $\calx$ and $\caly$ are measurable spaces, and that we are given a statistical model on $\calx \times \caly$ which, as you will recall from the first post, is a probability measure $Q$ on the cartesian product. For simplicity, we will assume that $\calx$ is a euclidean space $\bbr^n$, while $\caly$ is $\bbr$.

Furthermore, it will be convenient to assume that the statistical model on $\calx \times \caly$ is actually the joint distribution of a pair of random variables $X$ and $Y$. Precisely, we suppose that there is an ambient sample space $\Omega$, and that

\begin{equation}\notag X: \Omega \to \calx = \bbr^d \quad \text{and} \quad Y: \Omega \to \caly = \bbr \end{equation}

are random variables. We assume that the statistical model $Q$ is the joint distribution of $X$ and $Y$, which has a density with respect to the Lebesgue measure on $\bbr^d$, and either the Lebesgue measure on

Empirical risk minimization

Definition. Given a sample

\begin{equation}\notag S = \left\{ (x_i,y_i)\right\}_{i=1}^m \end{equation}

and a predictor $f:\calx \to \caly$, we define the empirical risk of $f$ to be

\begin{equation}\notag \widehat{\br}_S[f] = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m loss(f(x_i), y_i). \end{equation}

The empirical risk is an unbiased estimator of the true risk and more closely approximates the latter as the size of the sample grows. To formulate these claims more precisely, we must conceive of our sample as a realization of a sequence of iid random variables. Here are the details: We suppose that the original random variables $X$ and $Y$ are defined on a common sample space $\Omega$:

\begin{equation}\notag X: \Omega \to \bbr^d \quad \text{and} \quad Y: \Omega \to \bbr. \end{equation}

Together, these define a random variable

\begin{equation}\notag (X,Y): \Omega \to \bbr^d \times \bbr, \quad \omega \mapsto (X(\omega),Y(\omega)). \end{equation}

For each $i=1,\ldots,m$, we define the composite map

\begin{equation}\label{fire-eqn} \Omega^m \xrightarrow{\text{$i$-th projection}} \Omega \xrightarrow{(X,Y)} \bbr^d \times \bbr, \end{equation}

where $\Omega^m$ is the $m$-fold cartesian power of $\Omega$. By composing these maps with the natural projections

\begin{equation}\notag \bbr^d \times \bbr \to \bbr^d \quad \text{and} \quad \bbr^d \times \bbr \to \bbr, \end{equation}

for each $i=1,\ldots,m$ we get random variables

\begin{equation}\notag X_i: \Omega^m \to \bbr^d \quad \text{and} \quad Y_i: \Omega^m \to \bbr \end{equation}

such that the random variable

\begin{equation}\notag (X_i,Y_i): \Omega^m \to \bbr^d \times \bbr, \quad \omega \mapsto (X_i(\omega),Y_i(\omega)) \end{equation} is equal to the map \eqref{fire-eqn}. Furthermore, it’s easy to check that the random variables

\begin{equation}\notag (X_1,Y_1),\ldots,(X_m,Y_m) \end{equation}

are independent and $(X_i,Y_i) \sim (X,Y)$ for each $i$. Our sample $S$ is then conceived as a realization of this sequence of iid random variables.

Now, assuming that the loss function and the predictor $f$ are measurable, the sequence

\begin{equation}\label{sample-eqn} Z_1 = loss(f(X_1),Y_1), \ldots, Z_m = loss(f(X_m),Y_m) \end{equation}

of random variables is independent and identically distributed, with $Z_i \sim Z$, where

\begin{equation}\notag Z = loss(f(X),Y). \end{equation}

We then view our empirical risk as a realization of the random variable

\begin{equation}\label{average-mean} \cl{Z}_m \defeq \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m Z_i = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m loss(f(X_i),Y_i), \end{equation}

which is nothing but the sample mean of the random sample \eqref{sample-eqn}.

Theorem. The empirical risk, conceived as a realization of the random variable \eqref{average-mean}, converges in probability to the true risk. Precisely, for each $\dev>0$, we have

\begin{equation}\notag \lim_{m\to \infty} P \left\{\left| \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m loss(f(X_i),Y_i) - loss(f(X),Y) \right| >\dev \right\} =0. \end{equation}

In addition, the empirical risk is an unbiased estimator of the true risk.

The first claim regarding convergence is a direct application of the Law of Large Numbers, and, since the expected value of a sample mean is always equal to the population mean, the second claim is clear.
